title: HumorDB: Can AI understand graphical humor?
venue: 20th International Conference on Computer Vision, ICCV 2025 (Poster)
research_questions: Can AI systems understand graphical humor in images, as measured against human judgments?
How do state-of-the-art vision-only models and vision-language models compare with humans on binary humor classification, 1–10 funniness rating, and pairwise humor comparison?
Do minimally contrastive original/modified image pairs reveal whether models identify humor-inducing elements rather than superficial cues or editing artifacts?
Can model explanations, attention maps, and layer-wise analyses provide insight into how different architectures process visual humor?

datasets_used.name: HumorDB
datasets_used.size: 3,542 images; 1,271 pairs; 1,771 funny images and 1,771 not funny images

datasets_used.name: MSCOCO
datasets_used.size: 250 images in an external set of 650 non-humorous, non-edited images

datasets_used.name: Places365
datasets_used.size: 400 images in an external set of 650 non-humorous, non-edited images

methods_proposed.name: HumorDB
methods_proposed.summary: A curated visual humor dataset and benchmark containing photos, Photoshopped images, cartoons, sketches, and AI-generated images, with human measurements for binary, range-rating, and pairwise comparison tasks.

methods_proposed.name: Minimally contrastive visual humor pairs
methods_proposed.summary: The dataset includes 1,271 pairs of original humorous images and modified counterparts where the humor-inducing element is removed or altered while preserving visual similarity.

methods_proposed.name: Human-keyword-assisted VLM fine-tuning
methods_proposed.summary: For some fine-tuned vision-language runs, the authors prefixed prompts with words that appeared in at least 30% of human comparison-task responses for an image.

methods_proposed.name: Contrastive-pair attention analysis
methods_proposed.summary: For paired images, the paper approximates humorous regions using pixel-level differences between original and modified images and evaluates transformer attention maps with recall, strict box containment, and outside box ratio.

humor_theories.detail: The paper cites incongruity theory as the best-known theory of humor and motivates graphical humor as requiring expectations and identification of incongruities; it does not use a theory-specific annotation scheme.

evaluation_methods.detail: Online psychophysics experiments through Amazon Mechanical Turk and Prolific collected human labels for Binary, Range, and Comparison tasks; each participant rated 100 images, and each final image received 6-8 Binary or Range ratings or 4-6 comparisons.
VLM explanations were rated by humans on a 1–5 scale for identifying why an image is funny and for factual accuracy of the scene.

A separate in-lab validation study collected ratings for 400 images with >=5 ratings per image, yielding correlations with online data of rho = 0.78 for the binary task and rho = 0.72 for the range task.

Binary and Comparison tasks used accuracy against human-derived labels or majority votes; Range used RMSE against mean human ratings; control-set performance measured the percentage of external non-funny images classified as not funny.
Explanation alignment used All Words Score and Common Words Score based on stemming and synonym matching to human annotations.
Attention maps were evaluated with Recall, Strict Box Containment, and Outside Box Ratio; layer-wise logit attribution measured classification accuracy from each transformer layer.

headline_results.finding: The final HumorDB dataset is balanced and contains 3,542 images across 1,271 minimally contrastive pairs.
headline_results.value: 3,542 images; 1,271 pairs; 1,771 funny images; 1,771 not funny images

headline_results.finding: Human ratings validated the edits: original images were rated funnier than their modified counterparts for the majority of pairs.
headline_results.metric: original image rated funnier than modified counterpart

headline_results.finding: On the Range task, LLaVA with human-word fine-tuning achieved the best reported model RMSE on both test sets.
On the external non-humorous control set, the best reported model classified most images as not funny, supporting the claim that models were learning beyond editing artifacts.
headline_results.value: ViT-G14 (81%), LLaVA FT (80%), SwinV2 (76%), ViTHuge (71%), DinoV2 (62%)

headline_results.finding: In human evaluation of VLM explanations, Gemini received the highest ratings for both humor-source identification and factual accuracy.
Attention-map analysis showed limited localization of humorous regions even for transformer models.
headline_results.metric: attention Recall / Strict Box Containment / Outside Box Ratio
headline_results.value: ViTG-14: Recall Mean 0.265, SBC Mean 0.425, OBR Mean 0.348; DINOv2: Recall Mean 0.006, SBC Mean 0.552, OBR Mean 0.324

key_findings: HumorDB’s minimally contrastive pairs made the benchmark harder: the paper reports performance drops on testOnlyPairs and a 10% GPT-4o improvement when evaluating only original images, suggesting that modified images are an important control.

Pretraining mattered: pretrained vision models consistently outperformed non-pretrained models, while large vision-language models and fine-tuned LLaVA variants outperformed vision-only systems in the Binary task according to the paper’s reported trends.
The best Range-task model was LLaVA(words fine-tuned), with RMSE 1.68 ± 0.28 on testAllSet and 1.66 ± 0.31 on testOnlyPairs.

Image type strongly affected Binary performance: models were near chance on sketches, while Photoshopped and AI-generated images were easier; for example, Gemini-Flash scored 84 on Photoshopped images, 82 on AI-Gen images, and 53 on sketches.
VLM explanations could be fluent and sometimes aligned with human reasoning, but the paper shows cases where models hallucinated or missed the subtle distinction between paired images.
Attention maps and layer-wise analyses suggested that correct classification does not imply precise visual grounding: models often failed to focus on the small region that made the image funny.
limitations: The authors state that substantially enlarging HumorDB would likely improve performance but would require additional image modification, curation, and participant annotation.
Humor is culture-dependent, and the participant pool was predominantly from the United States (54%) and younger demographics (<40 years).
The benchmark uses majority votes and average ratings, which do not fully capture individual differences in humor perception.
Several original images came from the internet, and the paper notes that GPT-4o’s 10% improvement on original-only images suggests possible familiarity with similar images from training data.
The humorous region used for attention evaluation is approximated from pixel-level differences between original and modified image pairs, not from direct human segmentation.
safety_ethics_notes: The dataset construction removed images with explicit/offensive material and removed cases where humor relied on embedded text; the paper also notes cultural dependence and demographic skew in the participant pool.

artifacts.other: The supplementary material states that reference links for internet-collected images are provided in the repository.

human_samples.participant_count: Conflicting counts: Sec. 3.2 reports 650 online participants; Appendix 7.4 reports 850 participants total (200 Binary, 215 Range, 435 Comparison) and says some were discarded for reliability/outliers. Final retained N is not reconciled or reported.
human_samples.population: Crowdsourced online psychophysics participants. Reported demographics: age 20-29 47.0%, 30-39 30.3%, 40-49 13.8%, 50-59 5.2%, 0-19 2.1%, 60-69 1.6%; education undergraduate 56.3%, postgraduate 29.5%, high school 14.2%; gender male 52.6%, female 46.6%, non-binary 0.8%.
human_samples.geography: Nationality: United States 54.0%, South Africa 9.0%, Other 41 countries 37.0%.
human_samples.observations: Main text: each participant rated 100 images in one of Binary, Range, or Comparison tasks. Appendix instructions say 100-120 images per survey. Final images received 6-8 Binary or Range ratings or 4-6 comparisons. Repeated images were 10% of trials; Fig. 5 reports 1,800 repeated Range rating pairs. Comparison participants also gave brief justifications/words.
human_samples.detail: Human data supplied the benchmark labels/ratings: Binary funny/not-funny, Range 1-10 funniness, and pairwise Comparison majority choices. Quality controls included 500 ms minimum viewing time, repeated images, outlier removal, textual justifications for Comparison, and prevention of repeated participation in the same task. Original images were rated funnier than modified counterparts for 86.4% of pairs.

human_samples.population: Separate in-lab, non-crowdsourced participants; demographics and participant source not reported.
human_samples.observations: Ratings for 400 images with at least 5 ratings per image.
human_samples.detail: Used to validate crowdsourced annotations. Reported correlations with online data were rho = 0.78 for the Binary task and rho = 0.72 for the Range task. Participant count was not reported.

human_samples.population: Human evaluators of VLM explanations; population not described.
human_samples.observations: Rated VLM explanations on a 1-5 scale for how well they identified why an image is funny and for factual accuracy of the scene. Number of evaluators and number of explanation ratings were not reported.
human_samples.detail: These ratings support explanation-quality results such as Gemini Why Funny 4.1 ± 0.6 and Accuracy 4.24 ± 0.47, GPT-4o Why Funny 3.9 ± 0.7 and Accuracy 4.12 ± 0.63, and LLaVA Why Funny 3.5 ± 0.9 and Accuracy 3.57 ± 0.95.

human_samples.population: Study team/manual curators and image editors; exact personnel count not reported.
human_samples.observations: Constructed HumorDB from web-scraped images, AI-generated images, manual curation, Photoshop edits, and stable-diffusion-based inpainting. Final dataset has 3,542 images and 1,271 original/modified pairs.
human_samples.detail: Human curation removed explicit/offensive images and cases where humor relied on embedded text, capped each Google Search API result to 15 images, and created minimally contrastive modified counterparts by removing, obscuring, or replacing humor-inducing elements.

judges.name: Amazon Mechanical Turk and Prolific participants
judges.detail: Provided Binary labels, Range ratings, Comparison votes, and textual justifications/keywords used to create human-derived labels, mean ratings, majority votes, and word sets. Blinding to image condition/model identity was not specified.

judges.name: In-lab validation participants
judges.detail: Provided ratings for 400 images to validate online annotations; participant count, recruitment, and blinding were not reported.

judges.name: VLM explanation raters
judges.detail: Rated model explanations on 1-5 scales for humor-source identification and factual accuracy; rater count, assignment, item count, and whether raters were blind to model identity were not reported.

judges.name: Accuracy/RMSE/majority-vote scoring
judges.detail: Binary accuracy was computed against a human-derived label thresholded at mean Binary rating >= 0.5; Range used RMSE against mean human rating; Comparison accuracy matched majority human vote; external control-set performance was percentage classified as not funny.

judges.name: All Words Score and Common Words Score
judges.detail: Automated VLM explanation scoring used spaCy stemming and NLTK synonym matching against human annotation words, including all annotator words or words appearing in at least 30% of human responses.

judges.name: Attention rollout, difference-map masks, and logit attribution metrics
judges.detail: Attention maps were evaluated with Recall, Strict Box Containment, and Outside Box Ratio using pixel-level differences between original and modified pairs as a proxy for humorous regions. Layer-wise logit attribution computed binary classification accuracy from transformer-layer hidden states.

judges.name: Study authors/manual curators
judges.detail: Determined image inclusion/exclusion, image categories, and original/modified pair construction before human validation; the paper does not report independent blinded curation.

generation_selection.scope: AI-generated HumorDB images produced during dataset construction with DALL-E/MidJourney-like systems
generation_selection.detail: The paper says novel images were generated using AI-based models like DALL-E and MidJourney and then curated, but does not report prompts, number of generated candidates, number retained, sampling settings, or whether selection was best-of-N.

generation_selection.scope: Vision-language model answers for Binary, Range, Comparison, and example explanations
generation_selection.n: One answer per prompt is implied; Binary used two prompt variants whose results were averaged, not selected.
generation_selection.detail: No best-of-N generation, reranking, or self-selection is reported. For Binary VLM prompting, the paper used both 'Is the image funny?' and 'Is the image not funny?' and averaged results because performance was comparable. For Comparison, the prompt requested 'first' or 'second' plus a succinct explanation. For some figure examples, a suffix asked the model to start with yes/no and explain.

model_provenance.name: DINOv2 large
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a vision-only model; fine-tuned/evaluated locally. Exact checkpoint, implementation source, and pretraining source for this model were not specified beyond DINOv2 large.

model_provenance.name: ViT huge
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a vision-only model in pretrained and non-pretrained settings; exact checkpoint/source not specified.

model_provenance.name: ViT large
model_provenance.detail: Appears in category and attention-map results, although the prose model list emphasizes ViT huge. Exact checkpoint/source not specified.

model_provenance.name: Swin2/SwinV2 large
model_provenance.detail: Name appears as Swin2 large and SwinV2 large. Evaluated as a vision-only model; exact checkpoint/source not specified.

model_provenance.name: ConvNeXt large
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a vision-only model; exact checkpoint/source not specified.

model_provenance.name: ViTG-14
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a vision model; exact checkpoint/source not specified. The paper states pretrained vision models used weights from either ImageNet or LAION-2B, but does not map sources to every model.

model_provenance.name: ResNet152
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Models section and Range table. A Comparison-task figure axis transcription mentions ResNet50, creating an unresolved ResNet naming inconsistency.

model_provenance.name: LLaVA zero-shot
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated with VQA-style prompts. Exact LLaVA release/checkpoint not specified. Training-data mixture is described only generally by citing LLaVA papers.

model_provenance.name: LLaVA fine-tuned
model_provenance.detail: LoRA fine-tuned for HumorDB tasks. Exact base checkpoint and LoRA settings beyond grid-search/training details were not specified.

model_provenance.name: LLaVA words fine-tuned
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned with prompts prefixed by common human words appearing in at least 30% of responses for an image. Exact base checkpoint not specified.

model_provenance.name: BLIP fine-tuned
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned for HumorDB tasks. Exact BLIP checkpoint not specified.

model_provenance.name: BLIP words fine-tuned
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned with common human-word prompt prefixes. Exact BLIP checkpoint not specified.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated zero-shot and run one time. Access method, inference date, decoding settings, and cost were not reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Gemini 1.5-002 Flash/Gemini-Flash. Evaluated zero-shot and run one time. Access method, inference date, decoding settings, and cost were not reported.

model_provenance.name: DALL-E
model_provenance.detail: Used for AI-generated dataset images; paper text says DALL-E and reference list cites DALL-E 2, but the exact model/version and generation date are not specified.

model_provenance.name: MidJourney
model_provenance.detail: Used for AI-generated dataset images; exact model/version and generation date are not specified.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Training grid search over learning rates {0.01, 0.001, 0.0001, 0.00001}, batch sizes {4, 8, 16}, and weight decay {0.1, 0.01, 0.001}; Adam with weight decay; 10 epochs; cross-entropy for Binary/Comparison and mean-square loss for Range; validation-best checkpoint used for final test evaluation; LLaVA used LoRA fine-tuning; balanced sampling by rating bins for Range and Comparison.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Most experiments used 4 Nvidia GeForce RTX 2080 Ti GPUs on an internal cluster; LLaVA training and some Comparison-task models used an Nvidia A100 GPU. Monetary cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Each experiment was conducted 5 times for all models except GPT-4o and Gemini-Flash. Per-item inference-call counts, inference-time batch sizes, and decoding parameters for local VLMs were not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Binary used two prompt variants and averaged performance; Range used the 1-10 funniness prompt; Comparison used one two-image prompt requesting 'first' or 'second' plus a succinct explanation.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One run is reported for GPT-4o and Gemini-Flash; no multiple candidates, best-of-N, or reranking reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Access route, inference dates, temperature/top-p/max-token settings, retries, refusal handling, and API cost were not reported.

The paper reports use of DALL-E/MidJourney-like systems for novel images but does not report generation prompts, number of candidates, image-generation settings, cost, or selection rate. AI-generated images constitute 10% of the final dataset.

human_baselines.description: Human performance is shown alongside model bars in Binary-task figures. Humans performed the same funny/not-funny task on HumorDB images, and model accuracy was scored against human-derived labels thresholded at mean Binary rating >= 0.5.
human_baselines.detail: The task and images are materially matched, but the exact computation of the human accuracy bar relative to aggregate labels is not fully described, and the same human data also define the benchmark labels.

human_baselines.description: Table 3 includes a Humans RMSE row, and models are evaluated by RMSE against mean human 1-10 ratings.
human_baselines.detail: Humans and models both produced 1-10 ratings for the same task, but the paper does not fully specify how human RMSE was computed from individual ratings versus mean ratings.

human_baselines.description: Human performance is shown in Fig. 9, and model Comparison accuracy is scored against the majority human vote for image pairs.
human_baselines.detail: Humans and models both chose which of two images was funnier. The exact procedure for computing the human baseline from individual votes versus majority labels is not fully specified.

human_baselines.description: Human evaluators rated model explanations, and human annotation words were used for automated explanation alignment, but the paper does not report a human-written-explanation baseline evaluated under the same criteria.
human_baselines.detail: Humans act as judges/annotation sources rather than as a directly comparable explanation-generation system.

human_baselines.description: No human segmentation/localization baseline is reported; the humorous region is approximated by pixel-level differences between original and modified image pairs.
human_baselines.detail: Attention-map metrics are not compared with human attention maps or human-drawn humor-region masks.

mitigations: HumorDB is introduced as a new benchmark rather than a legacy public benchmark.
Train/validation/test splits are held out for supervised fine-tuning/evaluation.
Minimally contrastive original/modified pairs include newly edited counterparts that are less likely to appear verbatim in model pretraining data.
Subtle enhancements were applied to both original and modified images in a pair to reduce editing-artifact cues.
An external control set of 650 non-humorous, non-edited MSCOCO/Places365 images was used to test whether models simply learned editing artifacts.
contamination_risk.basis: The curated HumorDB benchmark and labels appear newly introduced, but many original humorous images were web-scraped from the public internet, and proprietary/open models may have been pretrained on similar or identical public images. The paper explicitly notes that GPT-4o’s roughly 10% improvement when evaluated only on original images suggests it probably saw original internet images or similar images in training. Modified paired images and held-out splits mitigate but do not constitute a decontamination test; exact image-collection dates, model training cutoffs, and overlap checks are not reported.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Human labels/ratings/majority votes and human explanation ratings are used; no LLM is reported as an evaluator or judge.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs are evaluated systems, not judges. Automated explanation scoring uses stemming and synonym matching, not an LLM judge.

reporting_gaps: Online participant count is inconsistent: 650 in the main methods versus 850 in the supplement, with no reconciled final retained count after exclusions.
Per-task retained participant counts after quality-control exclusions are not reported.
Crowdsourcing exposure count is inconsistent: main text says each participant rated 100 images; appendix instructions say 100-120 images.
Recruitment, demographics, and participant count for the in-lab validation study are not reported.
Participant count, recruitment, item count, assignment procedure, and blinding/model-identity masking for the human VLM-explanation evaluation are not reported.
Languages of participants and whether tasks were restricted to English speakers are not reported, despite English task prompts and textual justifications.
Exact open-model checkpoints, implementation versions, and pretraining-source mapping for each vision model are not specified.
The paper gives exact GPT-4o and Gemini model names but not access method, inference dates, decoding parameters, token limits, retries, or cost.
DALL-E/MidJourney dataset-generation prompts, versions, candidate counts, selection criteria, and generation dates are not reported.
No best-of-N generation is reported, but candidate counts and decoding settings are also not reported for VLM outputs.
Human baseline computation is under-specified for Binary, Range RMSE, and Comparison, especially because aggregate human judgments also define the target labels.

Dataset split counts in Table 1 are internally inconsistent: validation and testAll total columns do not match the funny plus not-funny counts, and train/valid/testAll totals do not sum cleanly to the stated total of 3,542.

Some model names are inconsistent across prose, tables, and figure axis transcriptions, including Swin2/SwinV2, ResNet152 versus figure-axis ResNet50, and figure-only labels not described in the Models section.
The external control set is described only as 250 MSCOCO and 400 Places365 non-humorous, non-edited images; detailed selection criteria and image IDs are not included in the supplied text.
Blinding is not reported for crowd raters, in-lab raters, manual curators, or explanation evaluators.
The paper cites likely exposure of GPT-4o to original internet images but reports no formal decontamination or image-overlap test.
